title: Your Life as Startup Employee #1 at Every Level (Unicorn or $0)
hook: You're hire number one. One and a half percent of a company worth six million dollars, or zero dollars if it fails in four years. Climb the company's real valuation ladder and learn the one thing every founder forgets to mention: the number on the cap table and the number in your bank account are never the same thing.
body: POV: you're an early startup employee — not the founder — riding a real, documented company-valuation ladder from a garage to a unicorn to the public markets. THE GARAGE HIRE: hire #1 gets a median 1.5% equity grant on a company worth about $6M pre-seed (real Carta 2024-2025 data), vesting over 4 years with a 1-year cliff. THE SEED CREW: a $15M seed round dilutes you overnight. THE PAPER MILLION: Series A closes near $78M — you're a 'paper millionaire,' which spends exactly like a real one, meaning not at all. THE SCALE-UP: Series B/C push the company to $400M, and growth-at-all-costs means executing the first layoffs yourself. THE DOWN ROUND: a rumored $1B term sheet collapses to a real $420M, and a real mechanic called a liquidation preference makes your on-paper stake worth $0 overnight. THE REBUILD, THE TENDER (a real, common practice: employees selling a slice of vested shares for actual cash before an IPO), THE LOCK-UP (the standard 180-day period after ringing the bell where you legally can't sell a share, no matter what the price does) — and the real ISO/AMT tax bite that shrinks the final headline number into the real one. A dramatized cautionary POV — the company and every named person are a fictional composite; the funding-stage valuations, dilution percentages, and equity mechanics are real and sourced. Watch the whole series — every life, every level, every way the climb costs you. For educational and entertainment purposes.
pinned: Here's the real mechanic that hits hardest: a 'liquidation preference' means new investors get paid back FIRST in a down round — so a company can be worth $420 million and your own vested stock can still be worth $0. Which level do you think costs the most: the down round, or the lock-up?
poll: Which level costs the most? THE GARAGE HIRE (below-market pay for a lottery ticket) / THE SCALE-UP (firing people you hired) / THE DOWN ROUND (your stake goes to $0 overnight) / THE LOCK-UP (rich on paper, still can't sell)

thumb.question: WHO ACTUALLY OWNS THE COMPANY YOU BUILT?
